summary: The post provides a comprehensive guide of interview questions candidates should ask to evaluate whether a potential manager and management culture will be healthy or toxic. Charity argues that candidates should treat interviews as a two-way evaluation, offering specific questions for both managers and engineers that reveal red flags like involuntary managers, lack of career development paths, and evasive communication styles.
key_points: Ask the same questions of multiple people and compare answers, giving extra weight to underrepresented minorities' experiences
The most revealing interview question is how work gets decided and allocated — evasiveness or pure top-down assignment are red flags
Test whether a manager can tell you 'no' directly during the interview as a trust signal
Management should be a lateral move, not a promotion, with viable paths back to engineering
Ask for specific situations rather than hypotheticals — any half-decent manager can BS on hypotheticals
money_quotes: You are all highly compensated subject matter experts who can find jobs relatively easily; there's no reason all the judgment and critique should flow in a single direction.

Most of all, can you get the manager to tell you 'no' on a thing you clearly want during the interview? Anyone who will look you straight in the eye and say 'no, and here's why', is someone you can probably trust to be straight with you down the line, in good times and bad.
Trust me, you never want to work for someone who was pushed into management against their will and still seems openly bitter about it.
A manager's One Job is to make sure that shit doesn't happen. It really is worth trying to find a good one you can trust.
Ask about specific situations. Any half-decent manager can BS on hypothetical stuff.
key_insight: Interviews are a two-way street, and candidates can reliably detect bad management culture by asking pointed questions about how work gets allocated, whether managers can say no directly, and whether management is treated as a peer role rather than a promotion.
